content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies about a person having to explore a dreamlike and surreal place

**Score:** 64
**Body:** I want a movie where someone (either unwilling or not) has to traverse an unreal and bizarre landscape, either the character is someone important and we explore why they do this or they're not important and it's just a pair of eyes to help us see the land before us, either could work, preferably a combination of both 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What Dreams May Come
2. Annihilation. 

Also the current movie Backrooms sounds like this. 
3. The Imaginarium of Dr Parnassus, Time Bandits, (both Terry Gilliam, the latter a much better film)
4. Mirror mask (2005)
5. Labyrinth
6. Spirited Away 
7.    Being John Malkovich (1999) fits your theme in an unusual way. 
8. Ink,
The Cell
9. Baron Munchausen, what dreams may come 
10. INCEPTION

## 2. looking for absurdly violent dark comedies!

**Score:** 30
**Body:** hello! i absolutely love absurd movies that combine violence, and revenge with comedy

im looking for films where a lot of terrible things happen but theyre presented in such an over-the-top, chaotic, and ridiculous way that you cant help but laugh. the more unhinged and outrageous the better

some of examples of this vibe for me are:

1. once upon a time... in hollywood
2. the boondock saints

i also really enjoy grindhouse style movies, exploitation films and etc. would love to get some recommendations!

UPDATE: in just a few minutes youve given me so many great movies to watch! thank you everyone for the help and for taking the time to stop by and recommend something!! <3

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cabin in the Woods
  - Tucker and Dale vs Evil as well, came out around same time afaik
2. Fargo
3. The Death of Stalin isn't entirely blood-filled, but has a lot of gratuitous death and the subject is a bit dark, and it's also a total comedy film, maybe funnier if you're into history, but funny regardless.
4. Tucker and Dale vs Evil (2010)
5. Shoot 'em up (2007) 
6. Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels

Snatch

Sexy Beast
7. Serial Mom starring Kathleen Turner.
8. Very Bad Things


Rough Night



In Bruges
9. Hott Fuzz, Sean of the Dead and The World's End

## 3. A movie that both romanticizes and deglamorizes loneliness

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Any genre is fine really, I guess I would prefer if the movie shows these themes from a single character point of view rather than several stories some good some bad.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Taxi driver 
2. The station agent for a slow, optimistic film shot loneliness
3. Moon (2009)

It's Sam Rockwell as a mining rig operator on the moon finishing a year long shift alone. 

Stunning cinematography in addition to the performance by Rockwell 
4. Perfect Days
5. Synecdoche, New York (2008) weaves between both perfectly
6. Castaway on the Moon

Lars and the Real Girl
7. American Splendor (2003)
8. Brian and Charles (2022)
9. Still Life (2013)
10. Drive (2011) 

Moon (2009)

Her (2013)

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind (2004)

Cast Away (2000)

Taxi Driver (1976)

## 4. Underrated Detective Movies & TV Shows?

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Any underrated or lesser-known detective movies/TV shows you'd recommend? Looking for hidden gems that most people don't talk about.


### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. M (1931)

Enola Holmes

The Thursday Murder Club

Out of the Past

Who Framed Roger Rabbit 
2. Prime Suspect

Cadfael

Cracker (Robbie Coltrane)

Sandbaggers

Callan (Edward Woodward) 
3. **SHOWS**

*Harrow (2018-21)*

*Prodigal Son (2019-21)*
4. Poker Face on Peacock. Charlie Cale is the role Natasha Lyonne was born to play.
5. Foyle's War
6. The blacklist “tv show” long legs “movie” longmire is another good detective show “country style” like the Yellowstone of detectives, copycat (1995), seven (1995)
7. Insomnia 

Darker than Amber 

The Stuntman 
8. Perry Mason with Matthew Rhys (the HBO reboot)
9. Citizen X (1995)

Mad Detective
10. River (2015) Miniseries with Stellan Skarsgard and Nicola Walker. Not the usual police procedural. Tight writing and smoothly directed. Stellan and Nicola are terrific, as always.  

Enola Holmes and Enola Holmes 2. Sherlock's little sister. Lots of fun, first rate cast

Wind River (2017) solving the murder of a Native American woman

The Indian Detective (2017) miniseries starring comedian Russell Peters as a Toronto policeman trying to crack a case. Fast paced, very funny and a great cast.

## 5. Perfect Days (2023)

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Thank you to this subReddit and to all those people who suggested me to watch this movie.

It’s not your typical action or high tension movie. It is a simple day to day life based on a working class man. The film is slow and takes its own time to reveal the plot. But I promise you, you will enjoy every minute of the movie.

Perfect Days is like watching a calm river flowing.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes! I enjoyed it very much too. Definitely a gem from 2023
2. I felt the same way after watching Perfect Days. If you enjoyed its quiet appreciation of everyday life, I'd highly recommend Paterson and After Yang. Different stories, same peaceful and reflective feeling.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Euphoria fans declare finale was worse than Game of Thrones and Stranger Things

**Score:** 2816

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Nah. The whole season was terrible, that the finale was actually great by comparison.
  - Those Sydney Sweeney puppy scenes will live forever thought 
2. They really should’ve ended with the first season and us not knowing whether Rue ODed or not.
  - It’s funny you say that because ambiguous endings seem to be the least liked endings in tv show/movie endings 
3. It actually improved as the season went on. It started very poor.
  - Agreed, the season overall was trash, but the last few episodes were pretty decent.
4. Ok, the finale was bad, but NOTHING can EVER beat Game of Thrones in terms of a “bad” ending. GOT was a cultural zeitgeist for damn near a decade and you couldn’t go ANYWHERE without someone referencing it. After the final season, it basically disappeared off the face of the planet. No one talks about it and the rewatch ability factor is so out the window. At least with Euphoria, the first 2 seasons can be rewatched.
  - Exactly. Let alone whenever there's a not great finale -- it's instantly compared to GoT. They won't be comparing it to Euphoria --- It'll stay GoT. It's the gold standard of bad endings.
    - Bet the writers of How I Met Your Mother are glad!
  - I heard that Game of Thrones merchandise held by retailers became literal garbage overnight. It went from profitable, to not being worth the space it was taking up in storage. 

## 2. Scary movie - Any one excited for this?

**Score:** 695
**Body:** It seems like a fun spoof movie.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m happy the Wayans are back in the chair since Scary Movie 2. Scary Movie 1 is a classic parody film.

Let’s be honest though, the movie is still not going to hit unfortunately. I’m looking forward to it for nostalgia reasons, but parody movies, let alone even comedies, are not what they were in the early 2000’s in terms of box office.
2. i call it parody movies and i love them all

i try to collect them all.

3. I'm excited any time Chris Elliott gets screentime.
  - ![gif](giphy|To9Cx4JR4YgUM)
4. Hopefully it’s good. Scary Movies are parody films they don’t need to be perfect.
5. They're doing the "We're going to be so offensive!" Thing, so, if movie flops, they can say it was cancel culture. 
  - Ironically, whenever someone says that it makes me want to watch it even less. Not because I expect to actually be offended but because people who say that are usually talking about the most mid jokes you’ve ever heard lol
6. I was excited till the trailer came out. The movie could be good but the trailer is really awful. They also did one of those prank interviews where the Wayans brothers were feeding Anna lines to say and while she did a good job the lines they gave her were not funny at all. Either they lost their spark or they just are having a bad time marketing. 
  - I (hope) it's just them not wanting to show the deep cuts in the trailer and save them for the movie so you are just seeing B-reel stuff. When they started making movies 25+ years ago, it was not uncommon for a movie to show the best jokes in their trailers or basically summarize the whole movie, and that tanks office ticket sales because people like to be pleasantly surprised.
7. My wife is very excited for it, already bought our tickets. I am not looking forward to it at all, but I will take a  bunch of edibles before and do my best to enjoy it for her. 

## 3. What is a bad scene in an otherwise good or great film?

**Score:** 547
**Body:** [Marion Cotillard death in Dark Knight Rises](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T7kK6vvxQrs)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She's been interviewed about this and said that was maybe the worst take of the entire day, and she has no idea why Nolan used it.
  - I don't understand it as well.
2. The most non believeable on screen death ive seen. Love the movie but i laughed so hard when i first saw this scene.
  - I’m still trying to figure out what the character’s cause of death was. Was her neck broken? Did she suffer internal bleeding? Was it blunt force trauma? 
    - A giant chunk of jagged plot armor pierced her heart
  - What's even more baffling is that Nolan is famously meticulous, a perfectionist who isn't exactly constrained by budget or the number of takes. On top of that, Marion Cotillard is an exceptionally talented actress with a long list of acclaimed performances behind her.

So I genuinely struggle to understand how that ended up being the take they chose. Surely they shot multiple versions of the scene. Even the best actors can have an off moment or make mistakes, but are we really supposed to believe that this was the strongest take they had?
3. The movie sucked but at the time was acclaimed

![gif](giphy|cWcuVTug7FDSE)
4. Irishman - when two grandpas were kicking 
5. https://preview.redd.it/ovy9kebhgq4h1.jpeg?width=849&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=3de2140fb0ea56f1cf29105e37123ae2eb23b277
6. The CGI Scorpion King in The Mummy Returns.

## 4. Which movie series do you wish had received a third installment to complete the trilogy? I always wished Guillermo del Toro's Hellboy had gotten a third film.

**Score:** 500

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/vsnpq4ldfo4h1.jpeg?width=460&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=7d84dcf1d56538dd402b87e5d7e68199773ff996

The Raid 3. The first two films are some of The best action movies I’ve ever seen.
2. National Treasure
3. Golden Army was a work of art. I wish he had done more Hellboy films! 
  - Luke Goss has an underrated screen presence.

He was a solid bad guy in both Blade 2 and Golden Army. 
4. The RDJ Sherlock Movies. 

![gif](giphy|924Py5ozHB5lK)


5. Because they only made 2... and thats all.

https://preview.redd.it/94xymbxaho4h1.jpeg?width=3464&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=e34049ced9486ec9e169269c5a0d9ef6f9f301a7
6. Dredd, could have been an awesome series 
7. *Blade Runner*.

I heard they’re making a television series that takes place 50 years after the last film, but *Blade Runner 2049* was fantastic and I would have loved to see the replicant revolution.

https://preview.redd.it/s1qyz6xpho4h1.jpeg?width=700&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=a6d460622ec7544a0faa1b5845bda3a31275f2ec
8. Hellboy is such a good pick. Without ron pearlman either, it's not the same. The time has passed 
9. They should have made another Constantine.  It was the only cool version of hell we've ever had cmon

## 5. Is this movie really as bad as they say? Honestly to me it seems like a compilation of 2000s YouTube skits

**Score:** 366
**Body:** To those who watched it, how was it? Did you die?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The period scene has me dying every time.

\>Hello, 911? Yeah, my friend is bleeding out of her vagina! Why are you laughing at me?!?!
2. To this day, the home school skit still takes me out. „You dropped you’re books, fuck face!“
  - "hello Mrs miller... I'm the pretty girl" is such lunacy 
3. Unpopular opinion: it's pretty funny. It's a shit movie but it's pretty funny. If Dennis Quaid demanding Common let him suck his dick at gunpoint doesn't make you at least crack a smile, are you even living? 


Some of the skits are painfully unfunny (grocery store dirty talk, batman speed dating) but some are also pretty golden (nutsack chin, homeschool high school, first period). 


Make sure you watch the Dennis Quaid version. The version where he's replaced with middle schoolers is a massive downgrade. 
  - I guess I had just assumed that was the entire schtick of the movie. All of the skits are meant to be uncomfortable or cringe to some degree, but instead of a shitty movie with bad actors, they pulled in as many A-listers as possible.

 Honestly my least favorite part of the whole movie is the main story with the boys. I would rather just skip those parts and watch the clips 

Is a good movie? No, not really, but I think it's funny the first time you watch it. It's just a dumb fun ride. 
    - It's a very fun ride, more when you don't watch the version with the main story kids, but with Quaid
  - TIL there are multiple versions of this movie.
    - I watched one version while on acid, and when I watched it again sober (different version) I thought I’d entered a state of psychosis
4. You can find a lot to laugh at if you're not uptight.

I guess that's true in general though
5. The ONLY part of this movie I remember is Hugh Jackman's neck nutsack.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on THE DEAD ZONE (1983), directed by David Cronenberg

**Score:** 33
**Body:** I think this might be Cronenberg’s most grounded film up to this point in his career. Given that it’s about a guy with psychic powers, that’s saying something. But as much as I was swept up in the plot, I was equally taken along by the emotions of the main character—his confusion, bitterness, resentment, etc.

I’m sure that Cronenberg and his screenwriter deserve plenty of credit, but it also wouldn’t surprise me if the source material—ie, the novel by Stephen King—was also part of the reason why. And of course, there’s Christopher Walken who seems to bring an emotional seriousness just by default; like even when his character is grinning, you can feel the pain underneath.

Interestingly, if I hadn’t known who the director was, I don’t know if I would have guessed Cronenberg off the bat. THE DEAD ZONE shares the theme of “mutation” with his other films, but it’s way less visceral this time. The horror aspect is more psychological. It’s nevertheless very well done though; for example, he makes the visual manifestation of John’s paranormal abilities different almost every time, which is something he also did in THE BROOD (1979).

But might another filmmaker have also brought that kind of style and thoughtfulness? Absolutely. Meanwhile, that the narrative doesn’t take place in an urban center this time gives THE DEAD ZONE a very different feel from even Cronenberg’s most recent film prior to this one (1983’s VIDEODROME).

Yet this is so well-paced and, as I said, involving that I can’t help but hold the film in high esteem.

Bonus: If someone were to ask me if THE DEAD ZONE represents Walken’s best work as the lead, I would say that, for me, it’s a tie between this and his performance in KING OF NEW YORK (1990), although Walken does different things in each (Here he projects an almost saintliness, whereas in KoNY he gave off a cool, menacing vibe. Yet what's interesting is that in both, Walken never seems like he's making acting choice; he just embodies these two characters effortlessly).

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorite films and one of the few good King adaptation. 
Is up there with Eastern Promises as my favorite “toned down” Cronenberg films.
Walken is perfect. Sheen is a tad over the top as is his henchman but that actually makes it relevant today.
All the actors bring presence, and you can tell many had worked on stage and with very dramatic directors before…Brooke Adams, Zerbe, Loom and Skerritt. 
It’s a film that whenever I watch it, makes me want to live a simple life in New England yet also motivates me to do good and get involved in (not politics) my community. 
It’s gritty yet soft. It’s adult horror and childhood trauma. It’s fantasy horror that is so grounded, it feels ripped from a back page news article.
If it were made today it would be shot clean with too much CGI with look crisp and lifeless. The period and setting, the film stock and Cronenberg’s letting the story be real without much time spent in the supernatural makes it very human.
I look forward to it every year, as I watch it just after Halloween, before the Holidays rush in. 
  - I completely agree with your sentiment of the film feeling real and grounded despite the future-seeing elements, which is amazing I think, and I also would dread a modern remake due to the crispness and cleanliness I imagine it would be filmed with.

However, what do you mean when you wrote it is “one of the few good King adaptation.”? 

I guess I perceive The Dead Zone as a bit of a deep cut when it comes to Hollywood adaptations of King, when off the top of my head there’s Shawshank, Green Mile, Stand By Me, Misery, Shining, Carrie, and more recently It and Long Walk weren’t half bad. Those alone put him head and shoulders above most other writers who are adapted as much as he is, of which I’m not sure there are many. And I’m sure arguments could be made for more based on the sheer volume of people who like or have been influenced by what I perceive as “lesser adaptations” like Pet Cemetery, Salems Lot, Children of the Corn and what have you…

Did you mean strictly based on the number of times his works have been attempted to be adapted to film vs how “successful” those adaptations end up being?
2. THE ICE IS GONNA BREAK!!

I also love this film. I’ll mention up front I have not read the source material from King so I am unaware of what liberties Cronenberg and the script may have taken with the story. I would also grant that Walken’s acting and Cronenberg’s direction could have been directly responsible for elevating the material, based on my own ignorance of it.

What struck me most the first time I watched it, just last year as a matter of fact, was the prescience (no pun intended) of the narrative. Disregarding the fantastical elements and their causes that are experienced by Walken, which I respect for never being explained, I can’t help but see the movie as a mirror reflection of the USA’s current state of political affairs.

Where I wish it was rather a funhouse mirror, the scene of Martin Sheen with his finger on the nuclear button, put into a position of ultimate power and  allowed to the launch missiles due to his initial populist appeal and lack of resistance from his spineless cabinet at his cabin, is absolutely chilling. And his “Hallelujah, gentlemen” comment as his actions are apparently finalized, reads to me the same as the current administrations warped embrace of christofacism, combined with Trump’s narcissism, egotism, and obsession with a legacy he doesn’t truly care that he’ll never see the negative consequences of. 

I’ll also suggest that in the film’s resolution where Walken takes matters into his own hands to prevent nuclear Armageddon, I can unfortunately see in the current political landscape Trump being praised as a genius for doing what Sheen does to prevent himself from being stopped by Walken.
3. Its one of my favorite movies. A few years ago, i read the whole stephen king book and was very disappointed. For such a long book, it didn’t real capture the best parts of the movie. King seemed to linger on unneeded descriptions.  It made me appreciate the movie even more!
4. I think it’s a masterpiece. They somehow took a huge unwieldy novel and distilled it down to the essence of John Smith’s story. All grounded by an incredible, heartrending performance by Christopher Walken. He runs the emotional gamut from humble happy teacher to bitter survivor to emotional recluse to someone who accepts his fate (gift) as a reluctant hero. And he is supported by many other amazing performances: Martin Sheen, Brooke Adams, Herbert Lom, Tom Skerritt, Anthony Zerbe, Colleen Dewhurst. They all give their best. There’s a discussion that Walken and Lom have about Hitler that is a masterclass in writing, directing, and acting. It’s a simple scene set in a kitchen, but it just demonstrates how great a film can be when everything comes together. The fate of the world hangs in the balance in a small kitchen in Maine.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. A query regarding Werckmeister Harmonies by Bela Tarr
Can someone tell

**Score:** 3
**Body:** 

A query regarding Werckmeister Harmonies by Bela Tarr

Can someone tell me if **Uncle Lajos actually handed over the list to the mob**? (As mentioned in the wikipedia article)  
I re-watched the movie multiple times but didn't find any evidence of it.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Backrooms and The Role of Uncanny Spaces

**Score:** 3
**Body:** After watching Backrooms, I was incredibly fascinated by the manner in which the cinema went completely quiet each time a character entered the liminal space. I can’t help but think that this is the role of ‘Uncanny’ spaces within horror film. I guess I would describe these spaces as paradoxical: they are both familiar and surreal. They’ve been a feature of many horror films, think Annihilation and The Shining. But what is it that makes these spaces so fascinating and perplexing? I can only speak to my own experiences, but I believe an aspect of this fascination is our thirst for knowledge and truth, and the manner in which these spaces deny access to a logical understanding. As truth seekers, we seek an answer to spaces that defy a logic and appear fundamentally mysterious. Thus, every time we feel Clark turn a new corner, we look along with him with the utter desperation of seeking more information about the environment.

Although there are a myriad of ways to interpret the film (I think it’s an apt metaphor for psychological stuckness), I think the film is fundamentally about the limits of knowledge, and despite desperately wanting to know, we only have a half-sketched image.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. "The Hole"(2019) at second wiew it' s al another thing

**Score:** 1
**Body:** Sorry for the bad English but I don't speak it, so this comes from Google Translate

  
This is my interpretation of the film as a metaphor about society and consumerism.

Part one: the protagonist wakes up in the Pit, in the "system." In front of him is an old man, the platform descends, and there's the leftover food from those above. The young man says it's disgusting and that those below will have to eat the old man's leftovers. The old man replies that it's right, because those above are those above and those below are those below. Those above can crush those below simply because they're the ones above. The old man calls them sons of bitches, even though he himself does the same thing and accepts all this. He uses the same insult after spitting out the wine and saying that those above have certainly done the same. I saw the two characters as a representation of different generations within society. The old man represents the older generations, who, like him, have spent many months in the Pit, and they too have spent a long time in society. The old man perfectly accepts the system, agrees to be part of it and encourage it, but complains about those above him, complaining without doing anything to improve the situation. The young man represents the new generations (not just the current one, but all generations in general when they're young). He's just entered the system, society. He represents those progressive, sensitive young people who read and are slightly intellectual (just enough to be called such), but, like young people just entering society, he's enormously deluded. For the first few days, he refuses to eat leftovers, but then he gives in, crushed by the reality of things, like all young workers and citizens. When he asks the old man his story, he, without realizing it, perfectly explains the problem of consumerism. He says he'd seen a commercial on TV for a knife sharpener. They say it changes lives and is indispensable, he didn't need it at all, but he buys it. The next day, the same company airs another commercial for a knife that sharpens itself, rendering any sharpener absolutely useless, and then destroys the television. After being asked what he chose as an object to bring he shows the knife sharpening itself, so he bought it. He had seen the rottenness of the system, but he gave in anyway, because knowing the system is often useless, as one is still subjected to it.

  
Second part: They wake up at a very low level, the protagonist is tied up and the old man will slowly eat him, because he is old, otherwise he would surely die. In society, the elderly need the fruits of the labor of the new generations, they devour what the young do and, especially recently, they devour their money. After the old man is killed by the protagonist, his ghost haunts him, saying that he did not have the same respect as the old man had for him, and he is right, the sense of guilt is justified. This is not the solution, killing the older generations is something too radical, the young survive even with the old they devour, they are given a chance, a dignity, that is why the annihilation of the older generations is immoral, it is evil. In this part, the illusion, already weakened, is now completely shattered. He has now seen the table arrive completely empty, he almost no longer lives by principles, but does whatever is necessary to survive, completely giving in to the system.

  
Part Three: Now the protagonist wakes up with another companion, a woman who was part of the administration. She wants to create a way for everyone to survive through solidarity; she rations her own food, and is active in starting the revolution, but her contribution, though it seems significant and morally important, is actually completely useless. After seeking revolution through words, the protagonist threatens those below that he will shit on their food if they don't do as he says, demonstrating that revolution, as in society, can only be started by those who have power over what people have, through violence and threats, rather than by convincing them through intellectual dialogue. Immediately afterward, the protagonist says he cannot convince those above, because he cannot shit upward. Those in a more privileged position will never be able to accept a revolution, as those below have no power over them and can't force them to revolution, so they eat as much as they damn well please. The company says they came there to see what life is like in the pit, since they didn't know it, and that there are 200 levels.

  
Part Four:The protagonist wakes up again on another level, finding the woman committed suicide. The level is 202. The bosses have lied to those working in the administration. And the woman, both because, after realizing that she can't convince those above her, her revolutionary movements have now become completely useless, shattering the illusion of change, since it can't happen from a lower class, from being metaphorically the lower class of society, and because she has realized that the bosses have lied to her, causing her to condemn many more people to death than she believed, and because those who serve power can't stand it when they see how the people really live. For all these reasons, she can't do anything but take her own life. There's a small scene, which I don't know if my interpretation is correct, in which he eats the pages of his book. I think this is a vision of the fact that those who are poor and always have to worry about surviving cannot (even if they would like to) become educated. It's the visual version of the Italian expression "you can't eat with culture."

  
Part Five: The protagonist wakes up on Level 6, a truly good level. His companion wants to move up, but is contemptuously prevented by those on the upper level, demonstrating that those in a lower class cannot move up, not because it's physically impossible, but simply because those above don't want those below to move up. By now, the protagonist, after all his time in the pit, in the system, after everything he's seen, is completely numb; every shred of revolutionary spirit has vanished. Or so it seems, because he proposes a new revolution to his companion, a revolution vastly more violent and radical than any other, demonstrating that even those who have spent a long time in society, in life, can have the desire to make a revolution and change things, especially those, like him, who survived being at the bottom and moved on to being at the top. The view briefly shifts to the kitchen, where they've found a hair in the panna cotta, demonstrating that not even the administration is perfect. They take up arms and descend, killing those who refuse to eat only what they need. Then they meet a man, a wise man, who tells them that they're mistaken in both the method and the purpose of their revolution. They must be more polite and deliver a message, namely, an intact plate that reaches the top, demonstrating that the system can fail and that it isn't omnipotent. In this case, the plate is panna cotta. As they descend, they kill more and more people until they reach the on floor 51, where they begin rationing food, things seem to improve from there; they manage to provide food to those lower down, but less and less. They are seriously injured in a fight. They descend to the point where even that isn't enough food, giving two grown men simply a piece of watermelon to eat. They've discovered that there aren't 250 floors, as the protagonist had calculated, but many, many more, demonstrating that those at the top have created and know of floors where food would never, ever, reach them, needlessly condemning people to death, demonstrating that the poverty of some countries is often much worse than we think, and that the administration is crueler than we can imagine.

  
Final: They reach the end, floor 333. They expect no one to be there, but someone is there: a little girl, an innocent and pure being, the opposite of the protagonists, who are covered in blood. At first they hesitate to give her the panna cotta, but then they let her eat it, giving in to human compassion. Showing that sometimes charity can overcome the cruelty of a society that puts the weakest in the worst possible situations. However, she is the message, a message that the administration is not invincible, symbolically bringing an innocent being (the little girl) to the ground zero, demonstrating that the unity of the system's members can break the system. The protagonist doesn't go with her; he initially wants to, but realizes that his sins are too great to carry such a pure message, and so he stays downstairs, in the dark, satisfied with having symbolically defeated the system, having achieved a sort of victory—not real, but moral—proving that, despite all the bad things he's done, he's still better than those who command the system. The message finally gets through.

  
This is how I saw the film on my second viewing, which made me understand many things I hadn't even noticed before. Perhaps I've been a bit forced at times in my interpretations, perhaps giving too much meaning to things that don't have any. But this is my interpretation of this work.
